coredata.dc:title: Ratio fluorescence and smartphone-assisted colorimetry dual-mode detection of creatinine based on F, B, N-doped of red fluorescent carbon dots

coredata.dc:description:
Background Creatinine is a small molecule disease biomarker that reflects kidney function, accurate and effective detection of creatinine will play an important role in the prevention and treatment of diseases. Currently, commonly used creatinine detection methods are limited by expensive instruments, complex sample preparation, many interference factors from biological samples, and environmental factors that can affect the accuracy of the measurement. Therefore, developing a fast, simple, inexpensive, sensitive analysis method that can eliminate background interference and provide multi-detection modes has strong attraction and value.
Results Here, we prepared ratio fluorescence and smartphone-assisted colorimetry dual-mode platform for the detection of creatinine based on F, B, N-doped of red fluorescent carbon dots (HAc-CDs). HAc-CDs were synthesized via hydrothermal method using o-phenylenediamine and tetrafluoroboric acid. Under an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, HAc-CDs exhibit two emission peaks at 599 nm and 652 nm with full width at half-maximums of 43.8 nm and 43.3 nm, respectively. In the presence of creatinine, the fluorescence peak at 599 nm with orange light increases, while the peak at 652 nm with red light decreases. This phenomenon of creatinine-HAc-CDs is inferred to be aggregation-induced emission. The ratio of fluorescence intensity (F
652
/F
599 ) is linearly related to the concentration of creatinine range from 3.0 × 10−10 to 1.0 × 10−3 M with a detection limit of 0.084 nM. Additionally, a smartphone-assisted recognition creatinine intelligent detection system based on the color change of paper strips was constructed.
Significance Creatinine in urine can be dual-mode detected with ratio fluorescence and smartphone-assisted colorimetry based on the prepared F, B, N-doped of red fluorescent carbon dots. Dual-mode detection system enables convenient, reliable and visual detection for on-site applications, and it has reference significance for the development of other disease biomarker detection methods.

head.ce:title: Ratio fluorescence and smartphone-assisted colorimetry dual-mode detection of creatinine based on F, B, N-doped of red fluorescent carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmacy, Guangdong Pharmaceutical University, Guangzhou Higher Education Mega Center, Guangzhou, 510006, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmacy, Guangdong Pharmaceutical University, Guangzhou Higher Education Mega Center, Guangzhou, 510006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Creatinine is a small molecule disease biomarker that reflects kidney function, accurate and effective detection of creatinine will play an important role in the prevention and treatment of diseases. Currently, commonly used creatinine detection methods are limited by expensive instruments, complex sample preparation, many interference factors from biological samples, and environmental factors that can affect the accuracy of the measurement. Therefore, developing a fast, simple, inexpensive, sensitive analysis method that can eliminate background interference and provide multi-detection modes has strong attraction and value.

Here, we prepared ratio fluorescence and smartphone-assisted colorimetry dual-mode platform for the detection of creatinine based on F, B, N-doped of red fluorescent carbon dots (HAc-CDs). HAc-CDs were synthesized via hydrothermal method using o-phenylenediamine and tetrafluoroboric acid. Under an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, HAc-CDs exhibit two emission peaks at 599 nm and 652 nm with full width at half-maximums of 43.8 nm and 43.3 nm, respectively. In the presence of creatinine, the fluorescence peak at 599 nm with orange light increases, while the peak at 652 nm with red light decreases. This phenomenon of creatinine-HAc-CDs is inferred to be aggregation-induced emission. The ratio of fluorescence intensity (F
652
/F
599 ) is linearly related to the concentration of creatinine range from 3.0 × 10−10 to 1.0 × 10−3 M with a detection limit of 0.084 nM. Additionally, a smartphone-assisted recognition creatinine intelligent detection system based on the color change of paper strips was constructed.

Creatinine in urine can be dual-mode detected with ratio fluorescence and smartphone-assisted colorimetry based on the prepared F, B, N-doped of red fluorescent carbon dots. Dual-mode detection system enables convenient, reliable and visual detection for on-site applications, and it has reference significance for the development of other disease biomarker detection methods.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Creatinine, derived from the anhydride form of creatine, is a byproduct of the natural breakdown of muscle tissues and is excreted by the kidneys without being reabsorbed in the tubules. As a small molecule substance of significant clinical importance, creatinine can reflect the filtration function of the glomeruli. Due to elevated urinary creatinine levels are commonly associated with acute kidney injury (AKI) [1], acromegaly, hypothyroidism [2], diabetes [3], cardiovascular diseases [4], meat consumption and physical activity, the World Health Organization (WHO) indicates that the effective concentration range of urinary creatinine is 30–300 mgdL−1 [5,6]. Therefore, accurate and effective detection of creatinine will play an important role in the prevention and treatment of diseases.

Currently, conventional methods such as colorimetry [7], spectrophotometry [8] electrochemical methods [9] and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [10] can be used for creatinine determination. While the standard method for creatinine clinical testing is the colorimetric assay based on the Jaffé reaction, where creatinine reacts with picrate under alkaline conditions to produce an orange color [11]. This method is inexpensive and easy to operate, it can also overcome the disadvantages of some methods such as expensive instruments, complex operation and time-consuming, so it is widespread used in clinical laboratories. However, the Jaffé method is highly nonspecific, this is because the presence of glucose, bilirubin, cephalosporins, ascorbic acid, ketones, and many other compounds in the target sample can interfere with its colorimetric reaction [12].

In recent years, sensors based on nanomaterials have been used to detect creatinine. Immunosensor based on gold nanoparticles, boron doped MXene, polyaniline, and anticreatinine antibody and bio-mimic nanozyme electrochemical sensors based on Mxene, Au nanoparticles and molecular imprinted poly(thionine) films have been developed for the detection of creatinine [13,14]. Jia group constructed a turn-on metal organic framework fluorescence sensor based on the interaction between Al3+ and creatinine, detection limit of creatinine was 4.7 nM [15]. Kailasa group designed various nanotechnology strategies, such as La3+-doped Ti3C2 nanosheets, Eu3+-doped strontium vanadate perovskite quantum dots and red emissive copper nanoclusters synthesized with 2-mercaptopyrimidine to construct fluorescent probes for the determination of creatinine [16–18]. However, the presence of only one emission peak can lead to interference in the fluorescence signal of the sensor due to various inevitable factors. A ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric dual-signal sensor CdTe@UiO-66-PC-Cu had been constructed for the detection of creatinine with detection limits of 2.48 nM, which eliminate signal fluctuations, resulting in more reliable and accurate analytical results [19].

Carbon dots (CDs) are a novel class of nanomaterials with characteristic dimensions of less than 10 nm [20], exhibiting numerous unique physicochemical and photochemical properties such as low toxicity [21], environmental friendliness [22], tunable fluorescence emission [23] and high fluorescence quantum yield [24]. These attributes make them promising materials for applications in bioimaging, environmental detection, catalysis and clinical therapy [25,26]. In order to enhance their fluorescence performance and target selectivity, various precursors have been employed to synthesize CDs, including inorganicchemicals, small organic molecule, biomass, polymers, biomolecules, etc. Zhou et al. [27] used citric acid as a precursor to synthesize CDs, achieving enhanced fluorescence properties for bioimaging and sensing applications. Similarly, Chen et al. [28] utilized glucose to synthesize CDs with tunable fluorescence for diabetes detection. Using phenylenediamine with different substituted positions of amino groups as precursors, carbon dots with multi-emission peaks can be synthesized [29].

Obviously, CDs with hetero atoms doping can improve their optical, chemical, and electrical properties when compared to pristine CDs [30]. Nitrogen (N), boron (B), fluorine (F) and other elements are commonly used doping materials. Nitrogen element with the high electronegativity can adjust the band structure and energy levels of CDs, resulting in a wider range of optical response and enhanceing their light absorption and luminescence capabilities [31,32]. Boron atoms have the similar atomic radius to that of carbon, so the doping of boron can improve the defects of CDs. In addition, boron atoms are p-type dopants that can suppress nonradiative transitions through electron absorption effects, thus it can improve the optical properties, and the fluorescence intensity or quantum efficiency (QY) of CDs can be greatly enhanced [33]. Fluorine-doped carbon dots, contrary to the electron donor of N-group, due to the inherent electron-accepting group with the high electronegativity and electron-withdrawing tendency, has been demonstrated to be a reliable strategy for tuning fluorescence emission and improving QY [34]. Although B acts as a hole-type dopant while N and F serves as an electron-type dopant, when they co-doped in CDs, asymmetric spin and charge density will synergistically regulate the charge distribution within the CDs, reducing energy bands and improving its optical performance [35–37]. The“turn-on”single peak fluorescence probe based on nitrogen doped CDs and gold nanoparticles for the detection of creatinine with the detection of limit of 0.02 μg mL− 1 in human urine samples had been reported by Kim group [38].

Based on the above situation, we adopt the strategy of introducing N, B, F with electron-donating capabilities groups into the CDs structure to improve their performance, using o-phenylenediamine and fluoroboric acid as precursors to fabricate acetic acid functionalization red fluorescence carbon dots (HAc-CDs). HAc-CDs can exhibit two emission peaks at 599 nm and 652 nm with narrow full-width at half-maximum (FWHM) due to it can create new energy states and absorption domains through the n→π∗ transition. Creatinine can increase the fluorescence intensity of HAc-CDs at 599 nm and decrease it at 652 nm. When the concentration of creatinine increases, the solution of HAc-CDs displays from blue to pink in sunlight, and the fluorescence color shifts from pink-purple to deep red in UV lamp. Accordingly, a ratio fluorescence probe and visual methods for the determination of creatinine were established. Additionally, the utilization of smartphone-based color recognition software enables the conversion of fluorescence color information into digital signals. This feature greatly assists paper-based probes in achieving visual detection of creatinine in urine at-home. The novel, highly sensitive dual-mode determination method overcomes the limitations of previous systems and is particularly well-suited for on-site and visual detection of creatinine, offering a convenient and portable solution for practical applications in clinical and home settings.
Materials and instruments used in this study can be found in the Supplementary Materials.

First, o-phenylenediamine (0.1080 g, 1 mmol), HBF4 (2 ml), and deionized water (10 ml) were stirred at room temperature for 1 h until fully dissolved. The mixture was then transferred to a 50 ml Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated in an oven at 180 °C for 6 h. After naturally cooling to room temperature, a dark green solution was obtained. The obtained solution was transferred to a dialysis bag (MD44-3500Da) and dialyzed with deionized water for 2 days. Subsequently, the purified suspension inside the dialysis bag was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min to obtain a black solid. The solid was filtered and dried in an oven at 80 °C for 12 h to obtain black carbon dots, referred to as O-CDs. To synthesize HAc-CDs, 5 mg of the synthesized O-CDs were dispersed in 5 ml of acetic acid and stirred for 3 h to obtain HAc-CDs encapsulated with acetic acid. For comparison, excluding the use of HBF4, the synthesis steps are identical to those described in O-CDs, and the resulting carbon dots are named G-CDs.

The HAc-CDs prepared were dissolved in ethanol and sonicated to form a uniform suspension. The transfer paper, measuring 7.5 × 10 cm2, was meticulously cut into rectangular segments of 0.9 × 1.5 cm2. These segments were subsequently immersed in an ethanol solution containing the HAc-CDs suspension. After a 5-min immersion period, the paper segments were carefully removed and air-dried under ambient conditions. Subsequently, a series of different concentrations of creatinine standard solutions were dropped onto the paper-based sensor, then they were positioned in a dark environment and subjected to photography under a 365 nm ultraviolet illumination source. Then different colors were read out with smartphone-assisted.

Fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of HAc-CDs was determined using quinine sulfate as a standard reference. Fluorescence spectra of quinine sulfate and HAc-CDs were carried out under an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. To minimize interference effects, the absorbance of quinine sulfate and HAc-CDs remains below 0.1 at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The fluorescence and UV–Vis absorption spectra of quinine sulfate were detected under the same conditions. And the QY of HAc-CDs was calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence and RGB detection of creatinine with HAc-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectrum of HAc-CDs was measured under excitation at the wavelength of 360 nm with a slit width of 10 nm, and a scanning range of 500–800 nm. The analytical performance of HAc-CDs on creatinine was evaluated by adding a series of different concentrations of creatinine into a suspension of HAc-CDs dissolved in ethanol with a concentration of 0.01 mg mL−1, fluorescence spectra were scanned under the same conditions as that of HAc-CDs, fluorescence intensity was recorded at 599 nm and 652 nm. According to the relationship between the ratio of fluorescence intensity at the above wavelength (F 652 /F 599) and the creatinine concentrations, fluorescence detection of creatinine was achieved.

RGB value is obtained by extracting the color in the paper-based fluorescent probes through a smartphone color recognition device (Color Picker software (Achim Heynen Technology)). An equation correlating creatinine concentration with the R/B ratio was derived to facilitate the subsequent quantitative analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of creatinine in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The analysis of creatinine in actual samples was conducted using urine samples from healthy volunteers. Freshly collected urine was diluted 100 times with deionized water, centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min, and filtered. Before the testing process, different concentrations of creatinine were spiked into the diluted urine samples. The creatinine content in the urine samples was determined using the ratio fluorescence probe based on HAc-CDs and the paper-based fluorescent probes with smartphone-assisted. The reliability of these methods were confirmed by calculating the recovery.

The preparation and fluorescence properties of G-CDs, O-CDs and HAc-CDs are illustrated in Scheme 1 . Both G-CDs and O-CDs have been synthesized through hydrothermal reaction for 6 h at 180 °C using o-phenylenediamine (OPD) as a precursor. Additionally, tetrafluoroboric acid (HBF4) is used as a dopant and stabilizer for the preparation of O-CDs. Under excitation at 360 nm, a fluorescence peak at 536 nm with a half-width of 78.6 nm was exhibited for G-CDs, and it appears yellow under sunlight and green under UV light (upper right corner in Scheme 1). For O-CDs, a distinct fluorescence peak is observed at 599 nm, while a shoulder peak is observed at 652 nm at the excitation of 360 nm. The color of O-CDs appears purple-red under sunlight and exhibits orange fluorescence under UV light (middle right corner in Scheme 1) with the full width at half maximums (FWHMs) of 54.4 nm. Upon addition of acetic acid (HAc) and stirring, the obtained HAc-CDs exhibit two emission peaks at 599 nm and 652 nm under excitation at the wavelength of 360 nm, the color of it is bright purple-red and bright red in sunlight and under UV light irradiation, respectively (bottom right corner in Scheme 1). The FWHMs of the two emission peaks in HAc-CDs are 43.8 nm and 43.3 nm, respectively. Obviously, the performance of HAc-CDs is superior to that of O-CDs.

The possible reactions of G-CDs, O-CDs and HAc-CDs formation are illustrated in Scheme 2 . Under high-temperature and high-pressure conditions, o-phenylenediamine molecules polymerize to form G-CDs [39,40]. When HBF4 was doped into o-phenylenediamine, they were conducted a hydrothermal reaction at high temperature and high pressure, the strong electrophilicity of the BF4 group and the nucleophilicity of the amino groups in o-phenylenediamine induce a positive charge on the carbon matrix and enhance the reactivity of adjacent carbon sites, so the boron atom attacks the adjacent position of OPD to form a phenazine ring to stabilize by resonance delocalization [41,42]. The phenazine ring undergoes oxidation and polymerization, resulting in the formation of dimeric compounds. The further carbonization and aromatization of these polymer fragments will generate network-like graphitic nitrogen structures aromatic clusters [43], they will ultimately be converted into O-CDs. These modifications enhance radiative processes, bolster aromatic domain rigidity, and reduce non-radiative pathways [44]. Upon addition of HAc with stirring, dehydration occurs between the –COOH of HAc and the –NH2 of O-CDs, leading to the formation of HAc-CDs.

To observe the morphology of O-CDs and HAc-CDs, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterization was conducted. As depicted in Fig. 1 a, O-CDs exhibit irregular shapes, while HAc-CDs present uniformly distributed nano-sized particles (Fig. 1b) with particle sizes ranging between 0.5 nm and 4 nm (Fig. S1). Additionally, interplanar spacing of 0.236 nm (Fig. 1b inset) of HAc-CDs reveals good crystallinity corresponding to the (1 0 1) plane of graphite carbon.

Fourier-transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) shows the typical absorption peaks of O-CDs and HAc-CDs in Fig. 1c. C–H stretching vibration in the benzene ring is at 754 cm−1. C–B, B–F, C–N, N–H, CN stretching vibration is at 1082 cm−1, 1219 cm−1, 1313 cm−1, 1400 cm−1, and 1624 cm−1, resceptively. The peak at 1454 cm−1 is attributed to the CC stretching vibration in the benzene ring, which is consistent with previously reported literature [44–46]. Specifically, HAc-CDs exhibits a unique absorption peak at 1737 cm−1, which is attributed to the CO stretching vibration [47,48]. The disappearance of the N–H (1400 cm−1) bond indicates the dehydration condensation between the –COOH in HAc and the –NH2 on the O-CDs, forming a CO bond. However, C–B and B–F bonds at 1082 cm−1 and 1219 cm−1 respectively are absent in G-CDs (Fig. S2). FT-IR confirms the successful doping of N, B, and F in O-CDs and HAc-CDs.

The UV absorption spectrum of the mixture of OPD and HBF4 (Fig. 1d, black line) reveals a strong peak at 295 nm, corresponding to the π→π∗ transition within the aromatic ring framework [49]. G-CDs (Fig. S3) exhibits absorption peaks at 289 nm and 427 nm, mainly attributed to the π→π∗ transitions of the CC bond and n→π∗ transitions of C−N/CN, C−O/CO structures in the aromatic systems, respectively [50,51]. The synthesized O-CDs (Fig. 1d, red line) and HAc-CDs (Fig. 1d, blue line) display absorption peaks at 284 nm, arising from π→π∗ transitions of conjugated structures in the copolymer [52–54]. This suggests the formation of quinoline structures as a result of protonation under the conditions of HBF4 acidification [55]. A broad peak at 566 nm is assigned to the n→π∗ transition of CN [56], while the absorption peak at 578 nm is attributed to the vibration relaxation of B–F [57,58], indicating the successful doping of N, B and F. A new absorption peak appears at 630 nm (Fig. 1d, blue line), accompanied by a color change from purple to pink in the solution, attributed to the complexation between O-CDs and HAc [59,60], suggesting the formation of HAc-CDs.

X-ray Diffraction (XRD) (Fig. 1e) shows diffraction peaks corresponding to the (001), (101) and (003) planes of HAc-CDs, very narrow diffraction peaks indicate the highly ordered crystalline structure [61]. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. S4) exhibits three diffraction rings, which corresponds to the (001), (101) and (003) planes from the outside to the inside, respectively. The results confirm the consistency of the two analytical techniques [62,63]. In the Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS) (Fig. 1f), peaks at 1.74 keV, 0.9 keV, 0.04 keV, 0.3 keV and 0.5 keV are caused by B, F, N, C and O, respectively. This indicates that HAc-CDs have been successfully doped with F, B, and N.

The emission spectra of HAc-CDs at various excitation wavelengths are depicted in Fig. 2 a. When the excitation wavelength is 360 nm, the corresponding fluorescence intensity of the emission peak at 652 nm is the highest, so 360 nm is selected for the subsequent experiments. Furthermore, HAc-CDs display excitation-independent characteristics at different excitation wavelengths, attributed to relatively uniform surface states and size distribution, resulting in surface state emission [64].

Fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of HAc-CDs was calculated based on the equation.

Where:

Q is the fluorescence quantum yield; I represents the integral area of the emission spectrum at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm; A is the absorbance of the absorption peak corresponding to the excitation wavelength in the UV–Vis spectrum of the sample solution; ŋ denotes the refractive index of the solvent; s and r represent HAc-CDs and quinine sulfate, respectively.
QY of quinine sulfate (Q r) is 54 %. The values of each term in the above equation for quinine sulfate and HAc-CDs are depicted in Table S1, and QY of HAc-CDs was determined to be 38.59 %.

As depicted in Fig. 2b, the UV absorption spectrum of the creatinine does not overlap with the excitation spectrum of HAc-CDs, and no new absorption peaks can be seen. Moreover, fluorescence lifetime of HAc-CDs and HAc-CDs coexisting with creatinine was 3.15 ns and 2.91 ns, respectively, indicating a generally unchanged fluorescence lifetime (Fig. 2c). Meanwhile, the addition of creatinine may promote the aggregation or clustering of HAc-CDs (Fig. S5), enhancing intermolecular interactions, resulting in the increase at 599 nm while decrease at 652 nm in fluorescence intensity (Fig. S6). This may be due to that the aggregation effects restrict non-radiative transitions, resulting in an increase in fluorescence intensity in the aggregated state, particularly at the lower wavelength region, while weakening the higher wavelength fluorescence [65,66]. Thus, it is inferred that the luminescence mechanism of HAc-CDs coexisting with creatinine is aggregation-induced emission (AIE) [67].

To further confirm the mechanism of fluorescence generation, we used the Dmol3 module in the Materials Studio software and density functional theory (DFT) to study the optimal structure and electronic excitation properties of CDs [68]. Two optimized structures of O-CDs and HAc-CDs models were established for DFT analysis (Fig. S7). Furthermore, the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) have been calculated (Fig. 2g). The HOMO distribution of O-CDs and HAc-CDs is on the conjugated π surface, while the LUMO distribution of O-CDs is on the graphitized core and that of HAc-CDs is concentrated on the CN–C group (Schiff base cluster) and the CO group formed with HAc. The LUMO and HOMO levels of HAc-CDs are lower than those of O-CDs, and the energy gap between HOMO and LUMO is reduced (from 2.18 eV to 1.79 eV), indicating that the introduction of the CO functional group enhances the conjugation of CDs, facilitating greater electron delocalization within the system [69,70].

Compared to O-CDs, HAc-CDs exhibit a red shift and new peaks at 652 nm (Fig. 2d). To confirm the effect of protons on HAc-CDs, we investigated the fluorescence emission and visualization of HAc-CDs in different solvents including ethanol, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), acetonitrile, formamide, methanol, deionized water and acrylic acid (Fig. 2e). Under sunlight, the colors displayed from left to right are violet, blue, blue, magenta, pink, violet, dark blue, and light blue. Under a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp, they are translated as bright red, orange, red, orange, pink, orange, blue, and dark red, respectively. The absence of fluorescence in deionized water is attributed to the aggregation of CDs, resulting in significant fluorescence quenching due to excessive resonance energy transfer or π→ π interactions [71].

The fluorescence spectra of HAc-CDs in different solvents are depicted in Fig. 2f. When in a proton-free solvent (DMSO, DMF, acetonitrile), the peak of HAc-CDs at 652 nm disappears and transforms into an indistinct shoulder peak, while an emission peak with high fluorescence intensity appears at 599 nm, similar to that of O-CDs. In other proton solvents, HAc-CDs all generate an emission peak with higher fluorescence intensity at above 640 nm, which further confirms that protons can make the peak red shift. Furthermore, HAc-CDs dissolved in ethanol solvent exhibit two emission peaks at 599 nm and 652 nm, the peak at 652 nm is the most prominent compared to other protonic solvents. The reason may be that hydroxyl groups in ethanol form hydrogen bonds with the oxygen/nitrogen of HAc-CDs, and the activity of CO in the structure of HAc-CDs can be enhanced, facilitating the Schiff's base reaction with the –NH2 on creatinine to dehydrate and form an imine, and the formation of fluorophores that alter the molecular state of O-CDs. Thus, a narrowing of the bandgap for photon transitions is produced and enables the generation of single-photon and red fluorescence emission [71].

The photoluminescent (PL) properties of CDs and the interaction of CDs with drugs are highly dependent on the reaction conditions. The effects of reaction temperature and reaction time of CDs, solvent, pH values, and the interaction time between CDs and drugs were carefully examined by controlling these variables.

Reaction temperature ranges from 140 °C to 200 °C with an interval of 20 °C, reaction time ranges from 2 h to 8 h with an interval of 2 h (Figs. S8a–S8d), an emission peak can be observed at 599 nm. Reaction at 180 °C for 6 h was proved to be the optimal reaction condition for synthesizing O-CDs.

The influence of different solvents on O-CDs was investigated, and the results showed that HAc was the most suitable solvent. Detailed results and discussion can be found in Fig. S8f and “3. The effects of solvent” in the supplementary materials.

Britton-Robinson universal buffer solutions were utilized to investigate the effect of pH value on the fluorescence of HAc-CDs. The color changes under different pH conditions are shown in Fig. 3 a. Under sunlight, the color of the solution is from transparent and colorless, violet, pink hue to a transparent and colorless state. Under a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp, the fluorescence color of the solution is blue, dark red, light red, green and blue. Fluorescence spectra reveal that distinct fluorescence patterns are based on variations in pH. Within the entire pH range, the emission peak transitions from a small peak to the appearance of 652 nm and 599 nm double peaks, and finally the double peaks shift to blue (Fig. 3b).

These observed phenomena are associated with possible reaction mechanisms. When pH < 7, HAc-CDs are under acidic conditions, where [H+] strengthens the hydrogen bonds within HAc-CDs, effectively locking the structure and increasing the delocalization of the liquid crystal conjugated system. Consequently, the expanded conjugated structure leads to a narrower bandgap, triggering red fluorescence emission from HAc-CDs. Simultaneously, the formation of hydrogen bonds inhibits the rotation of surface oxygen-containing functional groups, thereby suppressing non-radiative decay pathways and enhancing fluorescence efficiency [72]. At pH > 8, the fluorescence peak undergoes a blue shift, followed by fluorescence quenching. This is likely due to the deprotonation of the oxygen-containing functional groups presented on the carbon dots' surface, indicating disruption of their cross-linked structure [73,74]. These properties suggest that the fluorescence characteristics of O-CDs are primarily determined by surface states, as revealed by previous studies [64]. pH 4 is selected as the optimal pH condition.

The reaction time between HAc-CDs and creatinine is shown in Fig. S9. It can be observed that when creatinine is added in the HAc-CDs solution, the emission peak at 599 nm increases while the peak at 652 nm decreases (Fig. S9a). After 15 s of interaction, the ratio of peak intensity at 652 nm and 599 nm remains stable (Fig. S9b). It can be inferred that the interaction between HAc-CDs and creatinine is very rapid and the selected reaction time is 15 s.

The stability of HAc-CDs is a crucial factor in evaluating the performance of fluorescent probes. As depicted in Fig. 4 a, the fluorescence intensity remained relatively unchanged within the first hour, and the ratio of fluorescence intensity at 652 nm and 599 nm showed minor variations (Fig. 4b). The ratio of fluorescence intensity (F
652
/F
599 ) is 90.12 % and 84.74 % of the initial value at 8 h and 12 h, respectively. After 7 days (Fig. 4b inset), HAc-CDs still exhibited fluorescence. The excellent fluorescence stability can be attributed to the successful surface doping of N, B, and F on HAc-CDs. Additionally, the HAc added contains O–H and CO, which form a conjugated ring structure with the –NH2 on the surface of O-CDs, enhancing the stability of HAc-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The analytical performance of HAc-CDs for creatinine
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 5 a, with increasing creatinine concentration, the fluorescence intensity at 599 nm increases and at 652 nm decreases. The color of HAc-CDs changes from pale pink to deep red with the increasing concentration of creatinine standard solution under UV light (Fig. 5a inset). Within the creatinine concentrations range from 3.0 × 10−10 to 1.0 × 10−3 M, log CCreatinine is inversely proportional to the ratio of F 652 /F 599 (Fig. 5b). The linear equation is F 652/F 599 = −0.364 × (log CCreatinine) + 3.058 (R2 = 0.9937) with a detection limit of 0.084 nM.

In addition, we also developed a paper-based sensor for assisting in the detection of creatinine according to the color change of HAc-CDs with creatinine. A progressive series of creatinine concentrations of 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 μM were sequentially introduced from left to right onto the filter paper segments (Fig. 5c). Under ultraviolet illumination, a discernible color transition from blue to red was observed. The RGB values [75] corresponding to each filter paper segment were extracted by a smartphone platform. This facilitated the derivation of a linear regression equation relating the ratio of red to blue (R/B) to the concentration of creatinine (Fig. 5d). The equation is: R/B = 0.01944 CCreatinine + 0.1326 (R2 = 0.9974). This validation underscores the capability of smartphone-assisted paper-based probes for the real-time and visual quantification of creatinine.

For comparison, other methods for detecting creatinine from different literature are listed in Table S2. It can be observed that the ratio-type fluorescent sensor we prepared has a wide linear range and a low detection limit. Meanwhile, smartphone-assisted paper-based probes transform the color to digital signals, making creatinine determination convenient and fast.

Selectivity is indeed a crucial parameter for ratio-type fluorescent probes because potential interfering substances may affect the fluorescence response of HAc-CDs to creatinine during actual sample detection. To validate the selectivity of the ratio-type fluorescent probe for creatinine detection, some substances including small molecules (glucose (GLU), lactic acid (LA), uric acid (UA), urea), amino acids (l-glutamic acid (L-Glu), d-tryptophan (D-trp), l-tyrosine (L-tyr), l-cysteine (L-cys)), metal cations (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+) and anions (Cl−, CO3 2−, PO4 3−) have been investigated.

As shown in Fig. 6 labeled as "without creatinine", the addition of creatinine significantly decreased the fluorescence intensity ratio (F
652
/F
599 ) of the HAc-CDs suspension, while individual interfering substances did not affect the fluorescence intensity ratio reduction. Furthermore, the coexistence of interfering substances did not interfere with the fluorescence detection of creatinine by the ratio fluorescence probe (Fig. 6, labeled as "with creatinine"). These results indicated that common substances in urine do not affect the detection of creatinine, demonstrating the excellent selectivity and interference resistance of the ratio probe.

HAc-CDs fluorescence probe was employed for quantitative analysis of creatinine in urine samples. The creatinine content in urine samples was measured using the ratio fluorescence method and the RGB method (Table 1 ). HPLC method had been used to evaluate the reliability of the developed ratiometric fluorescent probe. Detailed methods and creatinine detection results with HPLC in urine samples were shown in “4. HPLC method for the detection of creatinine” and Table S3 in the Supplementary Materials.

According to Table 1, the fluorescence method for detecting creatinine in urine shows a recovery ranging from 99.00 % to 100.03 % with the RSD of 0.03 %–2.70 %. The recovery of the RGB method for detecting creatinine was 100.00 %–100.04 % and RSD between 0.24 % and 0.33 %.

In this work, red fluorescence carbon dots, HAc-CDs, were successfully synthesized via an environmentally friendly and cost-effective hydrothermal method. The successful doping of F, B and N enabled HAc-CDs to exhibit dual emission peaks at 599 nm and 652 nm. A ratio fluorescence probe had been constructed given the ability of creatinine to enhance the fluorescence intensity of HAc-CDs at 599 nm and reduce that at 652 nm. The method demonstrated a broad linear range, low detection limits, and recovery ranging from 99.01 % to 101.00 %. These results validate its potential applicability in the field of biomedicine. Furthermore, the development of a smartphone-assisted paper-based visual detection method for urinary creatinine holds promise for future applications as a portable and user-friendly home testing paper.

Qiqing Chen: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Wenhao Liu: Methodology, Formal analysis. Zengcheng Du: Methodology, Formal analysis. Yulan Li: Investigation, Formal analysis. Chaoying Zeng: Investigation, Formal analysis. Mingfang Zhu: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Mengjiao Pang: Investigation.
